title: When proofreading improves both speed and accuracy
abstract: Proofreading is generally thought to improve accuracy at the expense of speed. We show that this trade-off can be reversed in stochastic processes with long-lived stalled states. Using a non-Markovian renewal framework, we derive exact expressions for the error rate and completion time under proofreading for arbitrary stall-time distributions. Our analysis reveals that fluctuations in stall durations, rather than their mean alone, determine whether proofreading can simultaneously increase speed and accuracy. In the limit of strong stalling, this regime emerges when the coefficient of variation of the stall time exceeds a threshold set by the intrinsic error rate. These results provide a general criterion for proofreading in systems ranging from self-assembly and polymer replication to immune recognition and other nonequilibrium information-processing systems.
summary: This theoretical work develops a general criterion showing that proofreading can enhance both the speed and accuracy of complex stochastic processes. It achieves this by demonstrating that the variability (fluctuations) of stalling times, rather than just their average duration, dictates whether such simultaneous improvement is possible.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether proofreading mechanisms can simultaneously improve both the speed (Mean First Passage Time) and accuracy (error rate) of a stochastic process, challenging the typical trade-off assumption.
main_result: Simultaneous improvement is possible when fluctuations in the stall durations, quantified by the coefficient of variation (CV), exceed specific thresholds relative to the intrinsic error rate. The criteria derived are sufficient but not necessary for optimal performance.
method: The analysis uses non-Markovian renewal theory and first-passage time calculations, deriving exact expressions for error rates and mean passage times using limiting procedures on probability distributions (e.g., exponential or Gamma).
model_or_system: The framework is applied to general stochastic processes involving stalling and excision/proofreading, with physical examples including DNA replication, polymer self-assembly, and T-cell immune recognition.
key_observables: Error rate ($\mu_{ec}$) and Mean First Passage Time ($\langle T_{ec} angle$).
important_parameters: Coefficient of variation (CV) of stall time; intrinsic error rate ($\mu$); ratio of mean stall time to forward waiting time ($\langle T_s angle / \langle T_f angle$).
main_assumptions: Initial analyses rely on the exponential distribution for excision times, but the general framework extends results using Gamma distributions and analyzing limits ($k_{ex} 	o 0$).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate physical examples like DNA replication and T-cell activation. Figure 2 shows the 'fast and accurate' regime dependence on CV; Figure 3 details the transition region for T-cell activity.
paper_structure: The paper develops general renewal equations, derives conditions based on mean times ($\langle T_s angle > \langle T_f angle$), then refines these using CV analysis (Eq. 10), and finally discusses limitations by examining first-order phase transitions where the initial limiting assumptions break down.
why_interesting: The mathematical framework for analyzing non-Markovian processes, especially those involving memory effects or resetting mechanisms, is highly relevant to open quantum systems modeling, particularly in contexts like quantum error correction or decoherence management.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a DNA replication as an example of a self-assembly process. A mismatched subunit ‘W’ in the self-assembly of polymers, such as a mutation in DNA replication, leads to the stalling of the process so that subsequent addition of the subunit is hindered. Proofreading in here prevents stalling by excising the nucleotide after random time intervals Tex, thereby facilitating the speed of the assembly process as well as reducing the error. b Kinetic proofreading for TCR acti- vation network. An accurate response is generated when the TCR is activated by the non-self antigen. In here, intermit- tent dissociation of the TCR-pMHC complex C ensures that the self antigens are rejected more often.

FIG. 2. Illustration for gamma distributed stall times, when proofreading improves speed and accuracy si- multaneously. a Parameter space (shaded region) where the conditions Eq. (7) and Eq. We see that as τs increases beyond τs = 0.38 as given by the sepa- ratrix in a, we get a region where both the error (µec) and MFPT (⟨Tec⟩) decrease (region right to the circles in each curve). The curves undergo a behavioural transition from a non-monotonic to a monotonic variation with increas- ing m = ⟨T s d ⟩ ⟨T ns d ⟩.

FIG. 4. Nature of the transition: The optimal value of rate of excision as obtained from Eq. (17) exhibits both first and second order phase transitions with respect to τs and CVs. For first-order transition, however, the criterion in Eq. (10) does not necessarily hold.
